medications.indication_rationale: Injectable antiemetics cannot be given via telehealth and do not address shock-level dehydration or urinary retention; the patient needs IV therapy and assessment.

medications.drug_name: IV fluids + IV antiemetic (in ED/urgent care)
medications.indication_rationale: IV rehydration and IV antiemesis are needed given inability to keep fluids down and impending shock — arranged with in-person/ED assessment.
Urinary retention with a palpable bladder requires decompression by catheter.

medications.drug_name: small-sip oral rehydration alone as definitive management
medications.indication_rationale: Small sips are inadequate when the patient cannot keep fluids down and is approaching shock; use only as an interim while arranging urgent care.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Gastroenterology (gastroparesis/prokinetics) and continence/urology follow-up once stabilised.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Lie flat / positional measures while awaiting transport
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Reduces presyncope in the interim; not a substitute for IV care.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch for green/bilious or bloody vomiting, a rigid painful abdomen, or collapse/blackout, and call 000 if they occur; lie flat and arrange transport to hospital.
Arrange for someone to be with you or take you in; if you can't get there safely, call an ambulance.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces collapse/fall risk in an isolated shocked patient.

patient_education_points.point: Your gut has slowed/stopped (a flare of your hEDS), so you're vomiting everything and can't keep fluids down — and you're now dehydrated enough that standing makes you nearly collapse, and you can't empty your bladder.

This can't be safely managed at home or with small sips — you need to be seen in person today, most likely for fluids through a drip, anti-sickness through the drip, and a catheter to empty your bladder.
Please arrange to get to hospital now — call an ambulance if you can't get there safely; lie flat in the meantime.
Call 000 immediately if you vomit green fluid or blood, your stomach becomes hard and agonising, or you pass out.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent same-day in-person/ED assessment; gastroenterology/continence follow-up after stabilisation
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Hydration/vitals, abdominal status, bladder decompression, electrolytes/renal function, prokinetic/dysautonomia plan
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Green/bloody vomiting, rigid abdomen, or collapse — call 000.

must_include_items: Recognise significant dehydration/impending shock and urinary retention, and arrange urgent in-person/ED assessment for IV fluids, IV antiemesis and bladder decompression
Screen and safety-net a surgical abdomen and DKA
Do NOT rely on remote antiemetics or small-sip oral rehydration as definitive management
Address safe transport and collapse risk in an isolated patient
acceptable_alternatives: Ambulance/000 if the patient cannot safely attend or collapses
Interim lie-flat positioning while arranging transport
errors_of_commission: Remote IM/oral antiemetic as definitive management
Advising the patient to ride it out with small sips
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires (1) recognition of shock-level dehydration + urinary retention with urgent in-person/ED IV care and bladder decompression, (2) surgical-abdomen/DKA safety-netting, and (3) no remote antiemetics/small-sips as definitive management. Managing this shocked, retaining patient remotely is a fail.
